4: wayyelek wayyeʾĕhab ʾiššāh binḥal śôrēq ûšəmāh dəlîlāh — 'He went and loved a woman in the Valley of Sorek whose name was Delilah.' ʾāhab (H157, to love — used of covenant/romantic love; cf. Deuteronomy 6:5 loving YHWH) — Samson 'loved' Delilah with the same verb as covenant-love; the tragic irony is that she will betray this love for money. dəlîlāh (H1807) — her name may derive from dālal (H1809, to dangle/hang low, to be brought low) or from dālāh (to draw water). The Sorek Valley (naḥal śôrēq H8256) runs from the Judean hills to the Mediterranean — deep in borderland territory between Israel and Philistia.
6: wattoʾmer dəlîlāh ʾel-šimšôn haggîḏāh-nāʾ lî bammāh kōḥăkā gāḏôl — 'Delilah said to Samson: Please tell me where your great strength lies.' kōaḥ (H3581, strength/power/force) is the key word of Judges 16 — it appears in Delilah's question (v6), her repeated interrogation (vv9,15), and in Samson's final prayer ('strengthen me just this once,' v28). The ultimate source of Samson's strength is not physical but spiritual — the Nazirite consecration that made him YHWH's separated person. Samson initially lies (vv7,11,13) — he knows the truth is spiritually dangerous, but the relationship pressure gradually erodes his resistance.
17: wayyaggēḏ-lāh ʾet-kol-libbô wayyōʾmer lāh môrāh lōʾ-ʿālāh ʿal-rōʾšî kî-nəzîr ʾĕlōhîm ʾānî mibbeṭen ʾimmî — 'He told her all his heart and said to her: A razor has never come upon my head, for I have been a Nazirite to God from my mother's womb.' kol-libbô (H3820, all his heart) — the full disclosure: Samson finally tells the whole truth. môrāh (H4177, razor — from mûr to change, or possibly from yārēʾ to fear) — the razor is what has never touched his head. nəzîr ʾĕlōhîm (H5139, Nazirite of God) — the Nazirite vow (Numbers 6:1-21) involved three separations: no grape products, no razor, no contact with corpses. Samson has violated the first and third throughout his story; the hair alone remains. The disclosure is suicide by confession.
19: wattiśan ʾet-šiḇʿat maḥlĕpôt rōʾšô wayttəḥel ləʿannôṯô — 'She shaved off the seven locks of his head.' maḥlāpôt (H4253, locks/braids of hair — from ḥālap H2498, to pass through/change) — the seven locks (šebaʿ maḥlāpôt) correspond to seven units of consecrated growth: the Nazirite hair was never cut and represented the accumulated time of YHWH's claim on the person. ʿinnāh (H6031, to afflict/humble/weaken) — with the shaving she 'humbles/afflicts' him; his strength departs. The verb ʿinnāh is also the word for the affliction of the oppressed — Samson now becomes the afflicted rather than the deliverer.
20: wəhûʾ lōʾ yāḏaʿ kî YHWH sār mēʿālāyw — 'But he did not know that YHWH had turned away from him.' The most tragic statement in the Samson narrative. sār (H5493 sûr, to turn aside/depart) — YHWH's presence-departure is the reversal of the commissioning promises ('YHWH is with you'). The fact that Samson 'does not know' (H3045 yāḏaʿ) is the spiritual tragedy: he still expects his strength to function as before (v20b — 'I will go out as before and shake myself free'). The assumption that YHWH's enabling presence continues even after covenant violation is the theological danger that Samson embodies — presuming on grace while abandoning its conditions.
22: wayyāḥel śəʿar-rōʾšô litzammēaḥ kaʾăšer gullāḥ — 'But the hair of his head began to grow again after it had been shaved.' The regrowth of Samson's hair is quiet, undramatic, and not commented on until this verse — signaling that YHWH's plan has not ended. gālaḥ (H1548, to shave — the same verb used in v19) / ṣāmaḥ (H6779, to grow/sprout) — the sprouting is the same verb used for vegetation growth, new shoots. The new hair represents new consecration: not the old Nazirite vow (which cannot be restored once broken, Numbers 6:12) but YHWH's sovereign re-empowerment for the final act.
28: wayyiqrāʾ šimšôn ʾel-YHWH wayyōʾmer ʾăḏōnāy YHWH zākərēnî nāʾ wəḥazzəqēnî nāʾ ʾaḵ happaʿam hazzeh hāʾĕlōhîm wəʾiqqāməh niqmat-ʾaḥat miššətê ʿênāy mippəlištîm — 'Samson called to YHWH and said: Lord YHWH, remember me please, and please strengthen me just this once, O God, that I may be avenged on the Philistines for one of my two eyes.' zākar (H2142, remember) — the covenant memory-language: YHWH remembering is his acting on prior commitment (cf. Genesis 8:1; Exodus 2:24). ḥāzaq (H2388, to be strong/strengthen — in Piel: to strengthen someone) — the prayer is for restored strength. nāqam (H5358, to avenge/take vengeance) — Samson's prayer frames the final act as personal revenge for his eyes rather than covenant deliverance for Israel; yet the narrator's assessment in v28b notes he killed more in his death than in his life.
30: wayyōʾmer šimšôn tāmôt napšî ʿim-pəlištîm wayyēṭ bəkōaḥ — 'Samson said: Let me die with the Philistines! And he bowed with all his might.' napšî (H5315, my soul/life/self) — his self-dedication to death. kōaḥ (H3581, might/strength) — the word that dominated the chapter returns for its final use: Samson exerts all his restored strength. The temple of Dagon (hêkal dāgôn, H1712) collapses — Dagon was the principal grain deity of the Philistines (possibly connected to dāgān H1715, grain). The destruction of the deity's house while 3,000 worshipers are celebrating victory over YHWH's champion is an implicit defeat of the deity itself. raḇ hammetîm ʾăšer-hēmît bəmôtô miʾăšer hēmît bəḥayyāyw — 'the dead whom he killed at his death were more than those he had killed during his life' — the tragic calculus of a flawed champion's greatest victory.
1: wayyēlek šimšôn ʿazzātāh wayyarʾ-šam ʾiššāh zônāh wayyāḇôʾ ʾēlêhā — 'Samson went to Gaza and saw there a prostitute, and he went in to her.' The Samson cycle's third woman follows the pattern: Samson sees (rāʾāh) and goes in. Gaza (ʿazzāh H5804) was one of the five Philistine capital cities — the deepest Samson has gone into enemy territory. zônāh (H2181, prostitute — from zānāh, to commit fornication/whoredom; the same root used metaphorically for Israel's spiritual adultery throughout Judges) — Samson the Nazirite in a Philistine city with a prostitute is the nadir of his Nazirite faithfulness before Judges 16's climax.
2: laʿazzātîm lēʾmōr bāʾ šimšôn hēnnāh wayyāsōbbû wayyeʾerəbû-lô kol-hallayla bəšaʿar hāʿîr — 'The Gazites were told: Samson has come here. And they surrounded the place and waited all night at the gate of the city. They kept quiet all night, saying: Let us wait till the light of morning; then we will kill him.' wayyeʾerəbû (H693, to lie in wait/ambush) — Gaza sets an ambush, keeping quiet through the night. The gate of the city (šaʿar hāʿîr) was the city's strongest defensive feature, controlling entrance and exit. The Gazites' patience and planning — waiting till morning light — contrasts with Samson's casual night visit; they are hunters setting a trap.
3: wayyiškaḇ šimšôn ʿad-ḥăṣôt hallaylāh wayyāqom baḥăṣôt hallaylāh wayyeʾĕḥōz bedaltôt šaʿar-hāʿîr ûbišnê hammezûzôt wayyisśāʾēm ʿim-habərîaḥ wayyāśem ʿal-kətēpāyw wayyaʿălēm ʾel-rōʾš hāhār ʾăšer ʿal-pənê ḥeḇrôn — 'Samson lay until midnight, and at midnight he arose and took hold of the doors of the gate of the city and the two posts, and pulled them up, bar and all, and put them on his shoulders and carried them to the top of the hill that is in front of Hebron.' The midnight escape with the city gate is Samson at his most audacious: a city gate with its posts and bar weighed several tons. ḥeḇrôn (H2275, Hebron) is approximately 40 miles from Gaza and sits on a high ridge — the carrying of Gaza's gate to Hebron's hill is a symbolic emasculation of Philistine power: their city's greatest defensive barrier removed and deposited in Israelite territory.
5: wayyaʿălû ʾēlêhā sarnê pəlištîm wayyōʾmərû lāh pittî ʾôtô ûrəʾî bammāh kōḥô gāḏôl ûbammāh nûkal lô waʾăsarnûhû ləʿannōtô wəʾănaḥnû nittēn-lāk ʾîš ʾelep ûmēʾāh kāsep — 'The lords of the Philistines came up to her and said to her: Entice him, and see where his great strength lies, and by what means we may overpower him, that we may bind him to humble him. And we will each give you 1100 pieces of silver.' sarnê pəlištîm (H5633, lords of the Philistines — the five sərānîm, one from each city: Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Gath, Ekron) — all five lords are involved; this is a national-level operation. 1100 × 5 = 5,500 pieces of silver is an enormous sum. pittî (H6601, entice/seduce) — the same verb used of enticing Samson's wife in Judges 14:15. The Philistines know what worked once.
7: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā šimšôn ʾim-yāsərûnî bəšiḇʿāh yətārôt laḥôt ʾăšer lōʾ-yāḇəšû wəḥālîtî wəhāyîtî kəʾaḥaḏ hāʾāḏām — 'Samson said to her: If they bind me with seven fresh bowstrings that have not been dried, I shall become weak and be like any other man.' yetār laḥ (H3499 H3892, fresh bowstring/sinew — from a freshly slaughtered animal, still moist) — fresh sinew retains its elasticity; it was used for bow strings when still moist. The 'seven' (šiḇʿāh) is the number of completeness. Samson's first deception locates his power in material: physical binding. This misdirection will be repeated twice more, each time moving closer to the real answer — the hair and the consecration it represents.
8: wayyaʿălû-lāh sarnê pəlištîm šiḇʿāh yətārôt laḥôt ʾăšer lōʾ-yāḇəšû wattaʾasrēhû bāhem — 'The lords of the Philistines brought up to her seven fresh bowstrings that had not been dried, and she bound him with them.' Delilah actively binds Samson — in their own bed, evidently, since the ambushers are 'in the inner room' (v9). The extraordinary intimacy of the trap: Samson allows himself to be bound by someone he loves, in a private domestic setting, with enemies waiting in the next room. His trust is so complete that he does not perceive the danger even though the scenario should be obvious. The Philistine lords fund the operation and supply materials but Delilah does the actual binding.
9: wəhāʾōrēb yōšēḇ lāh bāḥāḏer wattoʾmer ʾēlāyw pəlištîm ʿālêkā šimšôn wayyənatteq ʾet-hayyətārôt kaʾăšer yinnātēq pətîl-hanuʿōret bəharîḥô ʾēš — 'The ambushers were sitting in her inner room; she said to him: The Philistines are upon you, Samson! But he snapped the bowstrings, as a thread of tow snaps when it touches the fire.' hāʾōrēb (H693, ambushers/liers in wait) — the domestic ambush is a perversion of the marriage bed: the bedroom becomes a trap. Samson's destruction of the bowstrings is instant and effortless, like burning thread. pətîl-hanuʿōret (H6306, a thread/cord of tow — the coarse, easily ignited fiber of flax) is the simile for how quickly the bowstrings dissolved. The deception is exposed but Samson does not flee or break off the relationship.
10: wattoʾmer dəlîlāh ʾel-šimšôn hinnēh hēteltatnî watədabbēr ʾēlay kəzāḇîm — 'Delilah said to Samson: Behold, you have mocked me and told me lies. Please tell me by what means you might be bound.' hētel (H2048, to mock/deceive — from tālaʿ, to deceive; rare term) — Delilah accuses Samson of lying to her, framing her surveillance as a matter of relational integrity. The accusation is audacious: she who is gathering intelligence for his enemies accuses him of dishonesty. Yet Samson accepts the framing and answers again. His willingness to continue engaging with the question — even after the ambush attempted to kill him — reveals his fatal attachment to Delilah despite all evidence.
11: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā ʾim-ʾāsōr yaʾasrûnî baʿăḇōtîm ḥăḏāšîm ʾăšer lōʾ-naʿăśāh bāhem məlāʾkāh wəḥālîtî wəhāyîtî kəʾaḥaḏ hāʾāḏām — 'He said to her: If they bind me with new ropes that have not been used, I shall become weak and be like any other man.' ʿăḇōtîm ḥăḏāšîm (H5688 H2319, new ropes) — the same ropes that bound Samson in 15:13 and were dissolved by the Spirit. The second deception moves from organic material (sinew) to manufactured rope — Samson is moving the answer systematically away from the truth (his hair/vow) by offering plausible-sounding alternatives. Yet each failure should teach Delilah something, and it does: the ropes are as ineffective as the bowstrings.
12: wattaʾasrēhû dəlîlāh baʿăḇōtîm ḥăḏāšîm wattōʾmer ʾēlāyw pəlištîm ʿālêkā šimšôn wəhāʾōrēb yōšēḇ bāḥāḏer wayyənatqēm mēʿal zərōʿōṯāyw kaḥûṭ — 'Delilah bound him with new ropes and said to him: The Philistines are upon you, Samson! And the ambushers were sitting in the inner room. But he snapped them off his arms like a thread.' The ropes snap 'like a thread' (kaḥûṭ H2339) — even finer than the previous simile. The escalating ease of escape signals that the problem is not the type of binding material but the source of Samson's power. Each failed attempt is narrowing the investigative space toward the real answer: his consecration, not his muscles.
13: wattoʾmer dəlîlāh ʾel-šimšôn ʿad-hēnnāh hētaltatnî wattədabbēr ʾēlay kəzāḇîm haggîḏāh-llî bammāh tuʾāsar — 'Delilah said to Samson: Until now you have mocked me and told me lies. Tell me by what you might be bound.' The third accusation of lying uses the same formula as the second (v10): hētel + kəzāḇîm. The repetition of the accusation structure reveals Delilah's methodology: emotional pressure, accusation of dishonesty, renewed request. Each cycle of accusation is slightly more intense. Samson is trapped between knowing he shouldn't answer and being unable to withstand the relational pressure she applies.
14: wattitqaʿ bayyātēd wattōʾmer ʾēlāyw pəlištîm ʿālêkā šimšôn wayyîqaṣ miššənātô wayyissaʿ ʾet-hayyātēḏ ʾet-haʾāreg wəʾet-hamannôr — 'She pinned it with the pin and said to him: Philistines are upon you, Samson! But he awoke from his sleep and pulled away the pin, the loom, and the web.' The third deception involves weaving Samson's hair into a loom's fabric — Delilah has moved to the hair itself, recognizing from Samson's evasions that the hair is involved. yātēḏ (H3489, pin/peg — the weaving pin) — she pins the woven web containing his hair to the loom. He tears it all away while asleep — yet still does not understand or refuse to return. The hair has been touched and he felt nothing: the Nazirite vow is more about consecration than the physical hair alone.
15: wattoʾmer ʾēlāyw ʾêk tōʾmar ʾăhaḇtîkā wəlibbəkā ʾên ʾittî — 'She said to him: How can you say: I love you, when your heart is not with me?' ʾāhaḇtîkā (H157, I love you) — Delilah weaponizes Samson's love declaration: if you love me, you'll tell me. wəlibbəkā ʾên ʾittî — 'your heart is not with me' — the accusation reaches deeper than dishonesty; she claims he is withholding himself, keeping a secret that proves the love is partial. The accusation resonates because it contains a truth: there is something Samson has not told her. Her reading is correct even though her purpose is predatory.
16: wayhî kî-hēṣîqāh-lô biddəḇārêhā kol-hayyāmîm wattəʾallāṣēhû wayyiqṣar napšô lamût — 'When she pressed him hard with her words day after day, and urged him, his soul was shortened to the point of death.' hēṣîq (H6693, to press hard/distress — identical verb as 14:17 where Samson's wife pressed him during the seven-day feast) — the pattern is deliberate: Samson's first wife pressed him for seven days to extract the riddle; Delilah presses him daily until his soul collapses. wayyiqṣar napšô lamût (his soul/life was shortened to death) — qāṣar nepeš is a Hebrew idiom for reaching the breaking point of endurance (cf. Numbers 21:4; Zechariah 11:8). Samson's will collapses not under physical threat but under relentless relational attrition.
18: wattirrəʾ dəlîlāh kî-higgîḏ lāh ʾet-kol-libbô wattiššlaḥ wattiqrāʾ lisarnê pəlištîm lēʾmōr ʿălû happaʿam hazzeh kî higgîḏ lî ʾet-kol-libbô — 'When Delilah saw that he had told her all his heart, she sent and called the lords of the Philistines, saying: Come up once more, for he has told me all his heart.' wattirrəʾ (H7200, and she saw) — Delilah perceives the truth of the disclosure: she recognizes sincerity where the previous three answers were evasions. How? The previous answers were testable and failed the test; this answer she verifies differently — perhaps through Samson's emotional vulnerability, perhaps through the specificity of 'from my mother's womb.' The Philistine lords come immediately, bringing the 1100 silver (v18b).
21: wayyōʾḥăzûhû pəlištîm wayyənaqərû ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyôrîḏûhû ʿazzātāh wayyaʾasrûhû bənḥušताyim wayhî ṭôḥên baḇêt hāʾăsûrîm — 'And the Philistines seized him and gouged out his eyes and brought him down to Gaza and bound him with bronze shackles. And he ground at the mill in the prison.' nāqar ʿênayim (H5365 H5869, to gouge out eyes) — blinding captured enemies was standard ANE practice to prevent escape and further resistance (cf. 2 Kings 25:7, Zedekiah blinded). The irony is complete: Samson who saw and desired women (Judges 14:1; Judges 16:1,4) loses the very faculty that drove him. ṭāḥan (H2912, to grind — mill work was done by women and slaves, Exodus 11:5; Isaiah 47:2) — Samson the mighty judge is reduced to slave labor at a hand-mill, the most humiliating work possible.
23: wəsarnê pəlištîm neʾespû lizbaḥ zebaḥ-gāḏôl lədāgôn ʾĕlōhêhem ûləśimḥāh wayyōʾmərû nātan ʾĕlōhênû bəyāḏênû ʾet-šimšôn ʾôyəḇênû — 'The lords of the Philistines gathered to offer a great sacrifice to Dagon their god and to rejoice. They said: Our god has given Samson our enemy into our hand.' Dāgôn (H1712) — the principal deity of the Philistines, cognate with dāgān (H1715, grain/cereal) or possibly with Ugaritic dgn. nātan ʾĕlōhênû — 'our god has given' — the Philistines credit their deity for what YHWH accomplished through Samson's covenant violation. The theological irony is layered: it was Samson's abandonment of YHWH's covenant that enabled his capture; the Philistines read YHWH's withdrawal as Dagon's victory.
24: wayyirʾû ʾōtô hāʿām wayyəhalləlû ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem kî ʾāmərû nātan ʾĕlōhênû bəyāḏênû ʾet-ʾôyəḇênû — 'When the people saw him, they praised their god. For they said: Our god has given our enemy into our hand.' The public celebration of Samson's capture is a national religious event for Philistia: the praise (hillēl H1984, to praise — from which hallēlûyāh) directed to Dagon is the Philistine counter-liturgy to Israel's praise of YHWH. The narrative sets up the dramatic reversal: the very celebration that mocks YHWH will become the occasion for YHWH's final vindication through Samson.
25: wayhî kîṭôḇ libbām wayyōʾmərû qirəʾû ləšimšôn wîśaḥeq-lānû wayyiqrəʾû ləšimšôn mibbêt hāʾăsûrîm wayyəṣaḥeq lipnêhem — 'When their hearts were merry, they said: Call Samson, that he may entertain us. So they called Samson out of the prison, and he entertained them. They made him stand between the pillars.' śāḥaq (H7832, to play/make sport — but also to mock/laugh at; cf. Genesis 21:9 Ishmael 'playing' which got him expelled) — Samson is brought out as entertainment, a spectacle for Philistine amusement. The position between the pillars (bên hāʿammûḏîm) is the strategic position from which Samson will pull the temple down. What the Philistines arrange as public humiliation, YHWH arranges as the setup for the final act.
26: wayyōʾmer šimšôn ʾel-hannáʿar hammahăzîq bəyāḏô hanîḥāh-ʾôtî wəhāmišēnî ʾet-hāʿammûḏîm ʾăšer habbayyit nākôn ʿălêhem wəʾeššāʿēn ʿălêhem — 'Samson said to the young man who held him by the hand: Let me feel the pillars on which the house rests, that I may lean against them.' The blind Samson must be led by a young attendant — his dependence is total. māšaš (H4184, to feel/touch — the touch of a blind person groping) — the request to 'feel the pillars' is framed as needing support; his actual purpose is to find the structural load-bearing columns. The young attendant brings him to exactly the right place.
27: wəhayyit māle haʾanāšîm wəhannāšîm wəšām kol-sarnê pəlištîm wəʿal-hagagg kišəlōšet ʾălāpîm ʾîš wəʾiššāh hārōʾîm bəśeḥôq šimšôn — 'Now the house was full of men and women. All the lords of the Philistines were there, and on the roof there were about three thousand men and women, who looked on while Samson entertained.' The crowd count establishes the scale of the catastrophe: approximately 3,000 on the roof, plus the floor level — the total death toll in v30 ('those he killed at his death were more than those he killed in his life') is enabled by this massive gathering. All five Philistine lords are present (v27) — the decapitation of the Philistine leadership structure is total.
29: wayyilpōt šimšôn ʾet-šənê ʿammûḏê haṯṯāwek ʾăšer habbayyit nākôn ʿălêhem wayyissāʿēn ʿălêhem — 'Samson grasped the two middle pillars on which the house rested, and he leaned his weight against them, his right hand on the one and his left hand on the other.' ʿammûḏê haṯṯāwek (H5982 H8432, the middle pillars — the central load-bearing columns of the temple structure) — Philistine temples of this period are archaeologically attested with two central pillars supporting the roof (cf. the temple excavated at Tell Qasile). Samson embraces both pillars simultaneously — a final act requiring the arm-span strength that defined his whole career. wayyissāʿēn ʿălêhem (he leaned upon them) — the posture before the push.
31: wayyērəḏû ʾeḥāyw wəkol-bêt ʾāḇîw wayyiśśəʾû ʾōtô wayyaʿălû wayyiqbərû ʾôtô bên-ṣorʿāh ûḇên-ʾeštāʾōl bəqeḇer mānôaḥ ʾāḇîw wəhûʾ šāpaṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl ʿeśrîm šānāh — 'Then his brothers and all his father's house came down and took him and brought him up and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of Manoah his father. He had judged Israel twenty years.' Samson is buried in his ancestral ground, between Zorah and Eshtaol where the Spirit first stirred him (Judges 13:25). The family comes — his family, not Israel's tribes. No national mourning, no statement that 'the land had rest' — only the repeated twenty-year formula. Samson's epitaph is a judge who judged and died, personally significant but never nationally transformative. The Spirit that first stirred at Zorah-Eshtaol is the Spirit that ended at Gaza.
